subtopic_detail: STEMI ECG criteria - posterior MI recognition
stem: A 58-year-old man presents to a regional emergency department with 90 minutes of crushing central chest pain radiating to the left arm, associated with nausea and diaphoresis. He has a history of hypertension and is an ex-smoker. He took two of his wife's GTN sprays without relief. The nearest primary PCI centre is 45 minutes away by road. Vital signs show HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% on room air, RR 18. He is in distress but mentating well. The initial 12-lead ECG shows 1.5 mm ST depression in V1 to V3 with prominent R waves in V2, upright T waves in V1 to V3, and 1 mm ST depression in II, III and aVF. There is no anterior ST elevation. A right-sided and posterior lead ECG is performed and shows 1 mm ST elevation in V7, V8 and V9. High-sensitivity troponin returns at 320 ng/L (reference <14). He has no contraindications to fibrinolysis.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 92, BP 138/86, SpO2 98% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1.5 mm V1 to V3, dominant R in V2, upright T V1 to V3. Posterior leads V7 to V9 show 1 mm ST elevation. Inferior leads show 1 mm depression.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 320 ng/L (<14). FBC, UEC, glucose, lipids pending.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate reperfusion strategy?

options.text: Conservative medical management with aspirin, ticagrelor and enoxaparin only, with serial troponins
options.rationale: Posterior STEMI is a STEMI equivalent and mandates emergent reperfusion. Treating as NSTE-ACS is the classic anti-pattern of missing posterior MI because there is no anterior ST elevation; this option would only be correct if anterior changes were purely reciprocal in a confirmed non-STEMI presentation with negative posterior leads (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Transfer for primary PCI without giving fibrinolysis, accepting the 45 minute transfer delay plus catheter-lab activation time, with full anticoagulation en route
options.rationale: Primary PCI is preferred only when first medical contact to balloon is achievable within 120 minutes for transferred patients. With a 45 minute road transfer plus catheter-lab activation, that target will be breached. This would be the correct strategy if the PCI centre were <60 minutes away or onsite, in which case fibrinolysis is avoided (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Administer fibrinolysis with tenecteplase now, plus aspirin, clopidogrel and enoxaparin, then transfer for pharmaco-invasive PCI within 3 to 24 hours
options.rationale: Posterior STEMI confirmed by V7 to V9 ST elevation is a STEMI equivalent requiring immediate reperfusion. When primary PCI cannot occur within 120 minutes of first medical contact, the Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway is fibrinolysis (tenecteplase weight-banded), antiplatelets (aspirin + clopidogrel preferred with lysis), and anticoagulation (enoxaparin), followed by transfer for angiography within 3 to 24 hours regardless of clinical course.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Treat the changes as non-ST-elevation, give aspirin, ticagrelor and fondaparinux, and refer for angiography within 72 hours
options.rationale: The 72 hour invasive window is the NSTE-ACS pathway and does not apply to STEMI equivalents. This would be correct only if posterior leads were negative and the troponin/ECG profile fitted intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS rather than transmural posterior infarction (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Repeat ECG in 30 minutes and reassess; if persistent changes, then consider fibrinolysis
options.rationale: Diagnosis is already established by V7 to V9 ST elevation and positive troponin; further serial ECGs add no information. The classic anti-pattern is treating posterior STEMI as an evolving NSTEMI; deliberate delay costs myocardium (time-is-muscle). Serial ECGs are appropriate only when initial ECG is non-diagnostic and troponin is pending (Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Posterior STEMI presents with ST depression and tall R waves in V1 to V3 and is confirmed with posterior leads V7 to V9 showing >=0.5 mm ST elevation. It is a STEMI equivalent. When primary PCI is not achievable within 120 minutes of first medical contact, the Heart Foundation/CSANZ pharmaco-invasive pathway (fibrinolysis then transfer for angiography 3 to 24 hours) is indicated.
key_points: Posterior STEMI = ST depression V1 to V3 with dominant R, confirmed by V7 to V9 elevation >=0.5 mm
STEMI equivalents (posterior, de Winter, Wellens evolution, LBBB with Sgarbossa) need urgent reperfusion
Primary PCI within 120 min FMC-to-balloon; otherwise lyse then transfer 3 to 24 h
Clopidogrel (not ticagrelor) is preferred with fibrinolysis to limit bleeding
explanation.pearls: Always do posterior and right-sided leads when you see V1 to V3 ST depression with upright T waves and dominant R in V2 (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

subtopic_detail: NSTE-ACS - timing of invasive strategy and GRACE risk
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents to a tertiary metropolitan emergency department with three episodes of central chest pain over the last 12 hours, each lasting around 15 minutes, the last occurring 90 minutes ago at rest while she was reading on her couch. She has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 8.2%), longstanding hypertension, chronic kidney disease stage 3a (eGFR 52, baseline stable), and was admitted 18 months ago with a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction managed conservatively at that admission. Her current medications are aspirin 100 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg. She is an ex-smoker (quit 8 years ago, 25 pack-years prior) and has a sister with CABG at 64. Vital signs at triage are HR 88 regular, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% on room air, RR 18, afebrile. She is mildly distressed but well perfused with warm peripheries. Examination is unremarkable apart from a grade 2 soft systolic murmur at the apex without radiation. The 12-lead ECG shows 1 mm horizontal ST depression in V4 to V6 with biphasic T waves in V5 and V6; there is no ST elevation in any territory and no posterior or right-sided changes on additional leads. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 78 ng/L (reference <14) drawn at 1 hour after symptom resolution. There is no pulmonary oedema clinically or radiographically and no haemodynamic instability. The cardiology registrar has calculated a GRACE score of 158.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 88, BP 152/84, SpO2 97% room air, RR 18, afebrile.
data_table.ECG: ST depression 1 mm V4 to V6, biphasic T waves V5 to V6. No ST elevation.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 78 ng/L (<14) at 1 h post-symptoms. eGFR 52. HbA1c 8.2%.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate timing of coronary angiography?

options.text: Within 24 hours of presentation, as part of an early invasive strategy
options.rationale: Heart Foundation/CSANZ NSTE-ACS recommends invasive angiography within 24 hours for high-risk NSTE-ACS: GRACE >140, dynamic ST or T-wave changes, or troponin rise consistent with MI. She meets all three criteria. The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is reserved for very-high-risk features (refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic/electrical instability, mechanical complication), which she does not have.
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Within 2 hours of presentation, as part of an immediate invasive strategy
options.rationale: The 2 hour immediate invasive pathway is reserved for refractory ischaemia, haemodynamic or electrical instability, or mechanical complications. None apply here. This would be correct if she developed ongoing pain despite therapy, VT/VF, cardiogenic shock or acute mitral regurgitation (Heart Foundation/CSANZ NSTE-ACS).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Within 72 hours of presentation, as part of a delayed invasive strategy
options.rationale: The 72 hour window applies to intermediate-risk NSTE-ACS without dynamic changes or significant troponin rise. GRACE 158 plus dynamic ECG changes plus positive troponin places her in the high-risk group requiring intervention within 24 hours. This would be correct only if GRACE were 109 to 140 with no dynamic changes (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Selective invasive strategy guided by non-invasive ischaemia testing after discharge
options.rationale: Selective/ischaemia-guided strategy is reserved for low-risk NSTE-ACS (GRACE <109, no dynamic changes, negative troponin). The anti-pattern is downgrading risk because of frailty or CKD; high-risk biomarker and ECG features override (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Defer angiography given CKD stage 3a; manage medically and reassess at 6 weeks
options.rationale: CKD stage 3a is not a contraindication to angiography; high-risk NSTE-ACS with CKD has greater absolute benefit from early invasive management with contrast minimisation and peri-procedural hydration. KHA-CARI explicitly endorses angiography in this setting. This would only be correct in stage 5 CKD on conservative management with limited life expectancy (Heart Foundation/CSANZ; KHA-CARI).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Heart Foundation/CSANZ stratifies NSTE-ACS into four invasive timing categories: immediate (<2 h) for instability; early (<24 h) for high-risk; delayed (<72 h) for intermediate-risk; selective for low-risk. High-risk criteria include GRACE >140, dynamic ECG changes, or rise/fall of troponin meeting MI criteria.
key_points: GRACE >140 = high-risk NSTE-ACS, angio within 24 h
Refractory ischaemia, instability, mechanical complication = <2 h
Dynamic ECG and positive troponin push to early invasive
CKD is not a barrier; mitigate contrast risk instead
explanation.pearls: Treat 'NSTE-ACS' as a risk-stratified pathway not a single diagnosis; the timing decision is the assessable step (Heart Foundation/CSANZ).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: A 61-year-old man underwent successful primary PCI with a single drug-eluting stent to the proximal LAD 6 weeks ago for anterior STEMI. He has been compliant with aspirin 100 mg daily, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, and metoprolol 50 mg twice daily. He has no atrial fibrillation, no prior stroke, eGFR 78, and no prior bleeding. He attends cardiology review and asks about antiplatelet duration. Two weeks ago he was diagnosed with a 3 cm right colonic adenocarcinoma on screening colonoscopy and is scheduled for elective laparoscopic right hemicolectomy in 4 weeks. He has had no chest pain since the procedure.

data_table.Procedure: Primary PCI LAD, 1 DES, 6 weeks ago.
data_table.Medications: Aspirin 100 mg, ticagrelor 90 mg BD, atorvastatin 80 mg, perindopril 5 mg, metoprolol 50 mg BD.
data_table.Risk factors: No AF, no prior bleed/stroke, eGFR 78, no upcoming surgery.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate antiplatelet management plan around the upcoming hemicolectomy?

options.text: Continue aspirin perioperatively, stop ticagrelor 5 days before surgery, and restart ticagrelor as soon as surgical haemostasis allows (typically 24 to 72 hours postoperatively)
options.rationale: Within the first 6 months after DES PCI for ACS, stent thrombosis risk is highest; CSANZ and NHFA guidance recommend continuing aspirin perioperatively for most non-cardiac surgery and pausing the P2Y12 inhibitor for the shortest possible window (ticagrelor 5 days), then restarting promptly once haemostasis allows. Delaying elective surgery is preferable to lengthening the off-DAPT window when possible (Heart Foundation/CSANZ ACS 2016; eTG).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Stop both aspirin and ticagrelor 5 days before surgery and resume both after discharge
options.rationale: Stopping both antiplatelets within 6 months of DES PCI for ACS substantially raises stent thrombosis risk; aspirin should generally be continued perioperatively if surgical bleeding risk is acceptable (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Continue both aspirin and ticagrelor throughout the perioperative period without interruption
options.rationale: Continuing both antiplatelets through colorectal surgery markedly raises bleeding risk; the recommended strategy is single antiplatelet (aspirin) during surgery (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Bridge ticagrelor with prophylactic low molecular weight heparin from 5 days pre-op until restart of ticagrelor
options.rationale: Heparin bridging does not protect against stent thrombosis (a platelet-driven event) and adds bleeding risk; bridging is for atrial fibrillation or mechanical valves on warfarin, not antiplatelets (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

options.text: Proceed with surgery now without changing antiplatelet therapy because the stent was placed more than a month ago
options.rationale: Six weeks post-DES for ACS is still within the very high-risk window; the planned management must explicitly address P2Y12 inhibitor timing rather than ignore it (CSANZ).
source_refs: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

explanation.summary: Dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) after PCI balances stent thrombosis (catastrophic, often fatal) against bleeding (frequent, less commonly fatal). Australian default after ACS-driven PCI is aspirin plus a potent P2Y12 inhibitor (ticagrelor preferred over prasugrel over clopidogrel) for 12 months, then lifelong aspirin. After elective PCI for stable angina, DAPT is 6 months unless bleeding risk is high (then 1-3 months). Perioperative management of patients within 12 months of ACS PCI is high-stakes: ideally postpone any non-urgent surgery, otherwise continue aspirin and bridge with shortest possible P2Y12 cessation. Heparin or LMWH bridging does not protect against stent thrombosis and is not recommended. Restart P2Y12 inhibitor with a loading dose as soon as surgical haemostasis allows, usually within 24-72 hours.
key_points: Post-ACS PCI: 12 months DAPT then aspirin lifelong
Ticagrelor preferred over clopidogrel in ACS (not with lysis)
Shorter DAPT considered when bleeding risk is high (e.g., HAS-BLED, prior bleed, frailty)
Stable CAD PCI default is 6 months DAPT
explanation.pearls: For elective non-cardiac surgery after DES PCI, defer 6 months for stable disease and 12 months for ACS where possible; if surgery is urgent, continue aspirin, stop ticagrelor 5 days and clopidogrel 5-7 days pre-op, restart 24-72 hours postoperatively (CSANZ; ESC perioperative).

sources.label: Heart Foundation/CSANZ 2016 Australian ACS Clinical Guideline

stem: An 82-year-old woman attends her general practice for review of newly diagnosed non-valvular atrial fibrillation found on a 24 hour Holter for palpitations. Her history includes essential hypertension on perindopril-indapamide, ischaemic stroke 3 years ago with full recovery, and no diabetes or heart failure. She weighs 56 kg. Current medications: perindopril-indapamide 5/1.25 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol as required. BP 132/80, HR 78 irregularly irregular. Bloods today: creatinine 138 micromol/L, eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, HCO3 24. She has no prior bleeding and her HAS-BLED score is 2. She prefers an oral medication strategy without routine INR monitoring.

data_table.Risk factors: Age 67, female, HTN treated, prior TIA, no DM, no HF, no vascular disease.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril/indapamide 5/1.25 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, paracetamol PRN.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate stroke-prevention prescription?

options.text: Apixaban 5 mg twice daily as standard non-valvular AF dose
options.rationale: Apixaban dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily is mandated when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are present; she meets all three, so standard dosing would over-anticoagulate (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily based on age, weight, and creatinine criteria
options.rationale: Per AMH and NHFA/CSANZ, apixaban is reduced to 2.5 mg BD when any two of age over 80, weight under 60 kg, or creatinine over 133 micromol/L are met; she fulfils all three, so the reduced dose is required to balance stroke prevention against bleeding (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

options.text: Warfarin titrated to INR 2.0 to 3.0 with point-of-care monitoring
options.rationale: Warfarin remains effective but DOACs are first-line for non-valvular AF in Australia, especially when the patient prefers monitoring-free therapy; warfarin would only be preferred for mechanical valves or moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily with the main meal of the day
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban requires dose reduction to 15 mg daily at CrCl 15 to 49 mL/min; her eGFR of 38 mandates the reduced dose, so 20 mg is incorrect (AMH).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as sole long-term antithrombotic therapy
options.rationale: Aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in non-valvular AF; it has minimal protective effect against cardioembolic stroke with comparable bleeding risk to anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: Non-valvular AF stroke prevention: calculate CHA2DS2-VASc, anticoagulate when >=1 in men or >=2 in women (excluding sex). DOACs are first-line over warfarin. Apixaban dose reduction (2.5 mg BD) requires at least 2 of: age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 micromol/L.
key_points: CHA2DS2-VASc: CHF, HTN, Age >=75 (2), DM, Stroke/TIA (2), Vasc disease, Age 65 to 74, female
Anticoagulate AF if >=1 (men) or >=2 (women, excluding sex)
DOACs preferred over warfarin in non-valvular AF
Aspirin and DAPT are not stroke-prevention strategies in AF
explanation.pearls: Female sex contributes a point but is not an independent indication for anticoagulation (NHFA/CSANZ).

subtopic_detail: Rate vs rhythm control in AF with heart failure
stem: A 58-year-old man with HFrEF (LVEF 32% on TTE 4 weeks ago) and persistent atrial fibrillation diagnosed 7 months ago is reviewed in the dedicated heart failure clinic of a metropolitan tertiary hospital. He is on sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily, bisoprolol 7.5 mg daily, spironolactone 25 mg daily, dapagliflozin 10 mg daily and apixaban 5 mg twice daily, all at maximum tolerated doses with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records. Despite these guideline-directed therapies, he reports NYHA class III symptoms with breathlessness when flat-walking 50 metres on level ground and orthopnoea requiring two pillows at night, with one episode of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea in the past week. Resting heart rate is 78 in AF on the clinic ECG; 6 minute walk test today is 280 metres (down from 360 m at his last review 3 months ago). He had one direct current cardioversion 4 months ago, which restored sinus rhythm initially with relapse to AF within 6 weeks despite continuation of beta-blocker therapy. He has no significant comorbidities (no diabetes, no CKD, no prior stroke) and lives 30 minutes from a metropolitan electrophysiology centre with established AF ablation programme. Symptoms have not improved despite optimal heart failure medical therapy for 3 months and adequate rate control.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 32%, mild LA dilatation, no significant valve disease.
data_table.Medications: Sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 BD, bisoprolol 7.5 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, dapagliflozin 10 mg, apixaban 5 mg BD.
data_table.Functional: NYHA III, 6MWT 280 m, resting HR 78 in AF, BP 118/72.
data_table.Procedural history: DCCV 4 months ago, AF relapse at 6 weeks.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step to improve symptoms and outcomes?

options.text: Increase bisoprolol to 10 mg daily for tighter rate control and continue current management
options.rationale: Rate is already controlled at 78 with adequate beta-blockade and symptoms persist; tighter rate control has no mortality benefit and risks worsening fatigue/hypotension. This would be correct only if HR were >110 at rest with symptoms attributable to tachycardia, in lone AF without HFrEF (NHFA/CSANZ AF; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: Add amiodarone 200 mg daily after loading, for pharmacological rhythm control
options.rationale: Amiodarone is reasonable in HFrEF when flecainide/sotalol are contraindicated, but catheter ablation has superior survival, HF hospitalisation and quality-of-life outcomes in HFrEF + AF (CASTLE-AF). The anti-pattern is reflex amiodarone in HFrEF when ablation is feasible. Amiodarone would be the preferred rhythm option only if ablation is unavailable or patient declines (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: Repeat DCCV and load amiodarone for maintenance
options.rationale: Repeat DCCV without substrate modification is unlikely to give durable rhythm control after early relapse. This would be reasonable as a one-off bridge while planning ablation, or if a clearly reversible trigger (sepsis, thyrotoxicosis) has been corrected since the first DCCV (NHFA/CSANZ; CASTLE-AF).

options.text: AV node ablation with biventricular pacing
options.rationale: AV node ablation plus pacing is a salvage strategy for refractory rate-control failure when rhythm control is not pursued, and commits to lifelong pacing without restoring sinus rhythm. It would be correct only after failed AF ablation and failed antiarrhythmics with persistent intolerable symptoms, particularly with pacing-dependent CRT indication (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for catheter ablation of atrial fibrillation as the rhythm-control strategy of choice in his HFrEF
options.rationale: In symptomatic AF with HFrEF despite optimal GDMT and adequate rate control, catheter ablation reduces all-cause mortality and HF hospitalisation and improves LVEF, symptoms and quality of life (CASTLE-AF, CABANA HF subgroup, CASTLE-HTx). NHFA/CSANZ recommends catheter ablation for symptomatic AF where rhythm control is desired and is the preferred rhythm strategy in HFrEF over antiarrhythmic drugs. This patient has failed DCCV, has ongoing NYHA III symptoms, and is geographically accessible to an EP centre.

explanation.summary: Persistent atrial fibrillation in HFrEF creates a self-perpetuating loop: AF worsens cardiac function, and worsening LV dysfunction perpetuates AF. Restoring sinus rhythm in carefully selected patients can reverse the tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy component. CASTLE-AF and subsequent trials show catheter ablation significantly reduces all-cause mortality, cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in symptomatic AF plus HFrEF compared with rate control alone, and outperforms amiodarone. The NHFA/CSANZ guideline accordingly positions ablation as the preferred rhythm strategy in this population, provided guideline-directed medical therapy is optimised first (ARNI or ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2 inhibitor) and rate control is in place. Amiodarone is reserved for non-ablation candidates because of long-term thyroid, pulmonary, hepatic and ocular toxicity. AV node ablation with biventricular pacing is a salvage option for refractory rate control. Anticoagulation continues lifelong regardless of rhythm strategy.
key_points: HFrEF + symptomatic AF: catheter ablation > drug rhythm control (CASTLE-AF)
Optimise GDMT before declaring rhythm-control failure
Flecainide and sotalol are contraindicated in HFrEF
AV node ablation + CRT is salvage, not first-line
explanation.pearls: In symptomatic AF plus HFrEF on optimal guideline-directed medical therapy and rate control, catheter ablation reduces all-cause mortality and HF hospitalisation (CASTLE-AF) and is the rhythm-control strategy of choice over amiodarone (NHFA/CSANZ).

stem: A 79-year-old man with persistent non-valvular atrial fibrillation diagnosed 4 years ago has worsening chronic kidney disease with a current eGFR of 22 mL/min/1.73m2 (CKD-EPI), serum creatinine 218 micromol/L, and stable weight of 64 kg (BMI 22.4). His comorbidities include hypertension on perindopril, type 2 diabetes mellitus on basal insulin (HbA1c 7.1%), and a prior ischaemic stroke 2 years ago with mild residual right hemiparesis but functional independence. His calculated CHA2DS2-VASc score is 6 (age >=75 = 2, HTN = 1, DM = 1, prior CVA = 2) and HAS-BLED score is 3 (age >65, HTN, abnormal renal function). He has been on rivaroxaban 20 mg daily for 3 years, but his renal function has progressively declined over the past 6 months from an eGFR of 38 to the current 22. He is not yet on dialysis and his treating nephrologist estimates he is 12 to 18 months from requiring renal replacement therapy. He has no mechanical heart valve and no moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis on his last echocardiogram. He has had one prior epistaxis requiring nasal packing 18 months ago but no other bleeding.

data_table.Other: No mechanical valve, no significant MS, no active malignancy.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate anticoagulation strategy?

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban 20 mg requires CrCl >50 mL/min; the AMH dose for CrCl 15 to 49 mL/min is 15 mg daily, and AMH advises caution for CrCl <30. Continuing the standard dose risks bleeding from drug accumulation - a classic dose-overlooked-in-AKI anti-pattern. Continuing 20 mg is appropriate only if renal function recovers above CrCl 50 (AMH; NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Switch to apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily with periodic renal function monitoring
options.rationale: Apixaban is the DOAC with the most renal-impairment evidence and has the lowest renal clearance (~27%). He meets the apixaban dose-reduction criteria: at least two of age >=80, weight <=60 kg, creatinine >=133 micromol/L - he has age 79 (not quite >=80) and creatinine 218 micromol/L (yes). However the AMH/PI also reduces dose to 2.5 mg BD when eGFR is 15 to 29 regardless of the standard criteria. The NHFA/CSANZ 2018 AF Guideline supports apixaban as the preferred DOAC in advanced CKD over warfarin given lower bleeding and similar efficacy.

options.text: Switch to dabigatran 110 mg twice daily
options.rationale: Dabigatran is ~80% renally cleared and is contraindicated in Australia when CrCl <30 mL/min per the AMH. eGFR 22 places him well below that threshold. Dabigatran would only be appropriate if eGFR were >=30 (>=50 for full 150 mg BD dose), and never in dialysis (AMH; NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Switch to warfarin with INR target 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Warfarin remains acceptable in advanced CKD but DOACs (especially apixaban) are now preferred where eGFR is >=15 because of lower intracranial haemorrhage and comparable stroke prevention. Warfarin would be the correct choice only for mechanical valves, moderate-severe mitral stenosis, dialysis dependence, or DOAC intolerance/contraindication (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Stop all anticoagulation and refer for left atrial appendage occlusion
options.rationale: Left atrial appendage occlusion is reserved for absolute contraindication to long-term anticoagulation (recurrent major bleeding off therapy, prior intracerebral haemorrhage where rebleeding risk is unacceptable). He tolerates anticoagulation; only the agent/dose needs adjustment. LAAO would be correct only after a clear, persisting contraindication (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: DOAC selection in CKD is driven by renal clearance: dabigatran (~80%) > rivaroxaban (~35%) > edoxaban (~50%) > apixaban (~27%). Dabigatran is contraindicated below CrCl 30; rivaroxaban dose-reduces in CKD 3 to 4; apixaban is preferred in advanced CKD with the 2.5 mg BD dose criteria.
key_points: Dabigatran contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min
Rivaroxaban 15 mg daily for CrCl 15 to 49
Apixaban 2.5 mg BD if any 2 of: age >=80, wt <=60 kg, Cr >=133 micromol/L
Warfarin only for mechanical valves, significant MS, dialysis, or DOAC intolerance
explanation.pearls: Apixaban has the lowest renal clearance and best evidence in advanced CKD - the right DOAC for declining eGFR (NHFA/CSANZ; AMH).

subtopic_detail: HFrEF - four pillars guideline-directed medical therapy
stem: A 62-year-old man with newly diagnosed HFrEF (LVEF 28% on transthoracic echocardiogram performed during admission) presents to the heart failure clinic at a metropolitan tertiary centre 2 weeks after discharge from his index admission with acute pulmonary oedema. The admission echocardiogram showed global hypokinesis without focal wall motion abnormality, mild left atrial dilatation and no significant valvular disease. Cardiac MRI during admission excluded an infiltrative cardiomyopathy and showed no late gadolinium enhancement suggestive of myocarditis. Coronary angiography demonstrated non-obstructive coronary artery disease. He is in sinus rhythm. Current medications post-discharge are ramipril 5 mg daily, bisoprolol 5 mg daily, and frusemide 40 mg daily. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.4%, on metformin 1 g twice daily), no atrial fibrillation, no significant valve disease, eGFR 64, potassium 4.2 mmol/L, sodium 138 mmol/L, BP 122/76 supine and 118/74 standing, HR 64 sinus rhythm, and NYHA class II symptoms when walking up two flights of stairs. He has no history of angio-oedema, no symptomatic hypotension or dizziness, and no recent gout flare. His weight is 88 kg (down 4 kg since admission with diuresis) and he is keen to optimise medical therapy following written education from the heart failure nurse practitioner.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 28%, no significant valve disease, mild LA dilatation.
data_table.Vitals: BP 122/76, HR 64 sinus rhythm, NYHA II.
data_table.Current Rx: Ramipril 5 mg, bisoprolol 5 mg, frusemide 40 mg, metformin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next addition or modification to his heart failure regimen?

options.text: Switch ramipril to sacubitril/valsartan and add spironolactone 12.5-25 mg daily and dapagliflozin 10 mg daily over subsequent visits
options.rationale: NHFA/CSANZ 2018 (with 2022 update) recommends the four pillars of HFrEF GDMT: ARNI (or ACEi if ARNI not tolerated/available), evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2 inhibitor. Each agent independently reduces mortality in HFrEF. Switch ACEi to ARNI (washout 36 hours), add MRA when K <5.0 and eGFR >30, and add SGLT2i regardless of diabetes status. Bisoprolol can be uptitrated. This sequenced approach matches the current quadruple therapy paradigm.

options.text: Add digoxin 125 micrograms daily for symptom relief without optimising disease-modifying therapy with ARNI, MRA and SGLT2i first
options.rationale: Digoxin reduces HF hospitalisations but has no mortality benefit and is not foundational therapy; the anti-pattern is reaching for digoxin before completing the four pillars. Digoxin would be reasonable for persistent symptoms despite full GDMT, or for rate control in HFrEF with AF when beta-blocker is maximally tolerated (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add ivabradine 5 mg twice daily as a heart-rate-lowering addition to current beta-blocker therapy, with electrocardiogram review at four weeks
options.rationale: Ivabradine requires sinus rhythm with resting HR >=70 despite the maximally tolerated beta-blocker, plus optimised baseline GDMT. He is HR 64 with un-uptitrated bisoprolol. This would be correct only after bisoprolol uptitration plateaus with persistent HR >=70 in sinus rhythm and persistent NYHA II to IV symptoms (NHFA/CSANZ; SHIFT).

options.text: Increase frusemide to 80 mg daily and add metolazone 2.5 mg as required for fluid control rather than disease modification today
options.rationale: Diuretics treat congestion but do not modify mortality; he is euvolaemic and the priority is mortality-reducing GDMT. The anti-pattern is escalating diuretics in a euvolaemic HFrEF patient because LVEF is poor. Diuretic escalation is correct only when there is congestion (rising weight, peripheral oedema, raised JVP) (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Refer for cardiac resynchronisation therapy with defibrillator (CRT-D) without further uptitration of guideline-directed medical therapy
options.rationale: CRT/ICD is considered after >=3 months of optimal GDMT with persistent LVEF <=35% and appropriate QRS criteria (LBBB >=130 ms for CRT, structural cardiomyopathy meeting primary prevention ICD criteria). He has been on partial therapy for 2 weeks. Device referral now skips the reverse-remodelling chance from quadruple therapy (NHFA/CSANZ).

explanation.summary: The four pillars of HFrEF (LVEF <=40%) GDMT: ARNI (or ACEi/ARB), evidence-based beta-blocker (bisoprolol, carvedilol, metoprolol-XL, nebivolol), MRA (spironolactone/eplerenone), SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin). Each reduces mortality; aim to initiate all four within weeks then uptitrate.
key_points: ARNI > ACEi when tolerated; 36 h washout from ACEi
MRA needs K <5.0 and eGFR >30
SGLT2i used regardless of diabetes status
Devices (ICD/CRT) considered only after >=3 months optimal GDMT
explanation.pearls: Sequence matters less than getting all four pillars on board within weeks at low doses, then uptitrate (NHFA/CSANZ 2022 update).

subtopic_detail: HFpEF - SGLT2 inhibitor as first-line disease-modifying therapy
stem: A 73-year-old woman presents to a community cardiology clinic with progressive exertional dyspnoea over 6 months, now limiting her ability to walk to her letterbox without stopping. She has longstanding essential hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 7.6%, on metformin 1 g twice daily and gliclazide modified-release 60 mg daily), obesity (BMI 34, weight 92 kg), and moderate obstructive sleep apnoea well-controlled on nightly CPAP at 9 cmH2O with good compliance documented. She had a hospital admission 3 months ago for fluid overload requiring intravenous diuresis and was discharged on oral frusemide as needed. Her examination today shows BP 138/82, HR 76 sinus, JVP not elevated, mild bibasal end-inspiratory crackles, and no peripheral oedema. Transthoracic echocardiogram performed last week shows preserved LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, left atrial volume index 38 mL/m2 (dilated), septal e' velocity 6 cm/s, E/e' ratio of 14, and no significant valvular disease. NT-proBNP is 612 pg/mL (elevated for her age and BMI, after BMI correction). Her current medications are perindopril 10 mg daily, indapamide 2.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gliclazide MR 60 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. Renal function: eGFR 58, K 4.3 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L. There is no history of gout, recurrent urinary infection, or genital fungal infection. She is keen on therapies that will reduce hospitalisation and would prefer to avoid additional injectable medications.

data_table.Echo: LVEF 58%, grade II diastolic dysfunction, LAVI 38, E/e' 14.
data_table.Current Rx: Perindopril 10, indapamide 2.5, metformin, gliclazide MR, atorvastatin.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate addition to her management for heart failure outcomes?

options.text: Add sacubitril/valsartan 49/51 mg twice daily
options.rationale: PARAGON-HF missed its primary endpoint in HFpEF; sacubitril/valsartan has only a Class IIb role and is positioned behind SGLT2 inhibitors. This would be a reasonable add-on if LVEF were in the lower-normal range (HFmrEF) or after SGLT2 inhibitor is established and symptoms persist (NHFA/CSANZ HF).

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg daily
options.rationale: TOPCAT showed regional-subgroup-only benefit; spironolactone is a reasonable add-on but not first-line and SGLT2 inhibitors have stronger HFpEF evidence (EMPEROR-Preserved, DELIVER). MRA would be the next step after SGLT2 inhibitor in symptomatic HFpEF, watching K and eGFR (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add bisoprolol 2.5 mg daily
options.rationale: Beta-blockers do not have robust HFpEF mortality benefit and can worsen chronotropic incompetence (a key driver of exertional symptoms in HFpEF). They would be correct only for an independent indication (AF rate control, post-MI, symptomatic ischaemia) (NHFA/CSANZ).

options.text: Add empagliflozin 10 mg daily as first-line disease-modifying therapy for HFpEF
options.rationale: EMPEROR-Preserved (empagliflozin) and DELIVER (dapagliflozin) both demonstrated reduction in the composite of cardiovascular death and heart failure hospitalisation in HFpEF, driven primarily by reduction in HF hospitalisation. NHFA/CSANZ 2022 guideline update recommends SGLT2 inhibitors as first-line disease-modifying therapy in HFpEF irrespective of diabetes status. She also has type 2 diabetes, providing dual benefit. eGFR 58 is well above the threshold for initiation.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Chronic heart failure

options.text: Increase frusemide as required and emphasise weight loss and CPAP adherence only
options.rationale: Lifestyle measures (weight loss, CPAP, BP control) are essential adjuncts but withholding SGLT2 inhibitor misses a Class I-equivalent mortality/hospitalisation benefit. This combination alone would be correct only if SGLT2 inhibitors were contraindicated (recurrent DKA, severe genitourinary infection, eGFR <20 for initiation) (NHFA/CSANZ; EMPEROR-Preserved).

explanation.summary: HFpEF (LVEF >=50%) management: BP control, comorbidity treatment (sleep apnoea, obesity, diabetes), diuretics for congestion. SGLT2 inhibitors are first-line disease-modifying therapy with Class I evidence based on EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER. ARNI and MRA have a more limited role.
key_points: EMPEROR-Preserved + DELIVER: SGLT2i reduce HF hospitalisation in HFpEF
SGLT2i benefit is independent of diabetes status
ARNI (PARAGON-HF) and MRA (TOPCAT) have weaker HFpEF evidence
Aggressively manage comorbidities: HTN, AF, obesity, OSA, CKD
explanation.pearls: The HFpEF treatment landscape changed dramatically post-2021; the SGLT2 inhibitor is no longer optional in suitable HFpEF patients (NHFA/CSANZ).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Chronic heart failure

subtopic_detail: Acute pulmonary oedema - early NIV and IV nitrate
stem: A 78-year-old woman with known HFrEF (LVEF 30% on her most recent echocardiogram 4 months ago) and a background of long-standing hypertension, permanent atrial fibrillation rate-controlled on bisoprolol and apixaban, and chronic kidney disease stage 3a (baseline eGFR 48) is brought to a busy metropolitan emergency department by paramedic ambulance with severe dyspnoea. She has been progressively breathless over the previous 24 hours after dietary indiscretion at a family birthday and woke with acute orthopnoea 2 hours ago. On arrival to the resuscitation bay she is sitting upright bolt-upright, gasping, with marked accessory muscle use, tracheal tug, and visible pink frothy sputum at the lips. Vital signs at triage: HR 132 in atrial fibrillation, BP 192/108, RR 36, SpO2 86% on 15 L non-rebreather mask, afebrile at 36.6 degrees Celsius. Examination shows JVP elevated to the angle of the jaw, widespread bilateral fine inspiratory crackles to the mid-zones, gallop rhythm with audible S3, and cool peripheries with capillary refill of 3 seconds. ECG shows atrial fibrillation with ventricular rate 130 to 140 and no acute ischaemic changes. Chest x-ray shows bilateral alveolar infiltrates with Kerley B lines and bilateral pleural effusions. Initial high-sensitivity troponin is 38 ng/L (<14), creatinine 142 micromol/L (up from baseline 110), potassium 4.6, lactate 2.4 mmol/L. She has a current Resuscitation Plan documenting a wish for full active treatment short of invasive intubation if it can be avoided.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 132 AF, BP 192/108, RR 36, SpO2 86% on NRB 15 L, afebrile.
data_table.Examination: Sitting upright, pink frothy sputum, JVP to jaw, widespread crackles, gallop, cool peripheries CRT 3 s.
data_table.ECG: AF rate 130 to 140, no ST changes.
data_table.Investigations: hs-troponin 38 ng/L, Cr 142, K 4.6, lactate 2.4. CXR: bilateral alveolar oedema, Kerley B.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate management combination?

options.text: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus, IV morphine 5 mg for anxiolysis and venodilation, with low-flow oxygen at 4 L via nasal prongs
options.rationale: Routine morphine in acute heart failure is associated with increased mortality and ICU admission and is explicitly de-emphasised - a classic legacy anti-pattern. Low-flow nasal prongs cannot achieve adequate oxygenation at SpO2 86% on NRB. This option would not be correct in any modern APO scenario; morphine is reserved at most for terminal palliative dyspnoea (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular; NHFA/CSANZ).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Intubate immediately for invasive mechanical ventilation
options.rationale: Immediate invasive ventilation overrides her Resuscitation Plan favouring non-invasive options; the 3CPO trial showed NIV frequently averts intubation in APO. Intubation would be correct only after a NIV trial fails (rising CO2, declining GCS, persistent hypoxia) or with frank cardiogenic shock requiring sedation/airway protection (Therapeutic Guidelines; NHFA/CSANZ).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: IV digoxin 500 micrograms loading and IV metoprolol 5 mg for rate control
options.rationale: IV beta-blocker in decompensated HF with cool peripheries and elevated lactate risks haemodynamic collapse; digoxin has no role in acute hypertensive APO. This would only be appropriate if rapid AF were the dominant driver of decompensation with preserved perfusion and afterload already addressed (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Commence IV nesiritide and IV dobutamine infusion
options.rationale: Nesiritide is not available in Australia (the anti-pattern of US-textbook recall). Inotropes are reserved for cardiogenic shock with hypotension; this patient is hypertensive at 192/108 and needs afterload reduction, not inotropy. Inotropic support would be correct only with BP <90 systolic, evidence of end-organ hypoperfusion, and adequate filling (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

options.text: Commence BiPAP (e.g., IPAP 12 to 14, EPAP 6 to 8 cmH2O), IV GTN infusion titrated to BP, and IV frusemide 40 mg bolus
options.rationale: Hypertensive acute pulmonary oedema is afterload-mediated; the priorities are oxygenation/ventilation and afterload reduction. Non-invasive ventilation (CPAP or BiPAP) reduces intubation rates and improves dyspnoea and oxygenation (3CPO trial; NHFA/CSANZ). IV nitrate is the afterload reducer of choice in hypertensive APO (Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular). IV loop diuretic addresses congestion. The combination addresses oxygenation, preload, and afterload simultaneously. Avoid morphine.
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

explanation.summary: Hypertensive acute pulmonary oedema management: sit up, high-flow oxygen, NIV (CPAP or BiPAP) early, IV nitrate for afterload reduction, IV loop diuretic for congestion. Avoid morphine. Inotropes only for cardiogenic shock. Identify and treat the precipitant (AF, ACS, infection, non-compliance).
key_points: NIV reduces intubation in cardiogenic pulmonary oedema (3CPO)
GTN infusion is the afterload reducer in hypertensive APO
Loop diuretic addresses congestion; do not over-diurese into shock
Avoid morphine - higher mortality and ICU admission
explanation.pearls: BP 192/108 with APO = afterload problem; reduce afterload before chasing diuresis (Therapeutic Guidelines).

sources.label: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - Acute heart failure

subtopic_detail: Cardiac vs vasovagal syncope - high-risk features
stem: A 72-year-old man with hypertension and a 30 pack-year smoking history presents to ED after collapsing without warning while shopping. Bystanders report he became briefly stiff with a few arm jerks; he was unresponsive for around 30 seconds and recovered immediately on the floor with no confusion. He had two similar episodes in the past 2 months, one while sitting watching television. Examination: BP 142/82 lying, 140/82 standing, HR 60 sinus, no postural drop, normal cardiovascular and neurological exam. ECG: sinus rhythm 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB plus left anterior fascicular block. Bloods within reference. He drives a private car daily for his work as a courier.

data_table.ECG: Sinus 58, PR 220 ms, QRS 110 ms, RBBB + LAFB (bifascicular block).
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, glucose, hs-troponin, TSH all within reference.

lead_in: Which combination is most appropriate as his next steps?

options.text: Admit for telemetry and TTE, advise him not to drive a private vehicle until cause is identified and treated, and notify him of his obligation to inform the licensing authority
options.rationale: High-risk cardiac syncope features (age over 60, no prodrome, syncope while sitting, recurrent episodes, bifascicular block) mandate admission with monitoring and echocardiography per ESC 2018. Austroads Assessing Fitness to Drive 2022 imposes a 4-week non-driving period for unexplained syncope at minimum, longer if cardiac, and the driver bears the duty to inform the licensing authority of any condition affecting fitness to drive (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Admit for telemetry and TTE but allow private driving once telemetry is normal for 48 hours
options.rationale: Austroads requires defined non-driving periods after unexplained or cardiac syncope based on aetiology and treatment, not on a brief in-hospital telemetry result; this would breach the standard (Austroads).

options.text: Discharge with reassurance, advise hydration and counterpressure manoeuvres for vasovagal syncope, and continue driving
options.rationale: His features are atypical for vasovagal (no prodrome, sitting, recurrent, abnormal ECG) and high-risk for cardiac syncope; discharge and continued driving are unsafe (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

options.text: Arrange outpatient CT brain and EEG for suspected new-onset seizure disorder and advise no driving for 6 months under epilepsy rules
options.rationale: Brief myoclonic jerks during cerebral hypoperfusion (convulsive syncope) are common and do not indicate epilepsy; rapid recovery without confusion argues against seizure, so epilepsy driving rules are misapplied (BMJ Best Practice).

options.text: Discharge home for outpatient 24-hour Holter and tilt-table testing within 4 weeks; continue driving in the interim
options.rationale: Outpatient deferral and continued driving with high-risk features and bifascicular block exposes him to in-vehicle syncope and third-party harm; admission with monitoring is the standard (ESC; Austroads).
source_refs: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

explanation.summary: Syncope assessment uses risk stratification to separate the benign (vasovagal, situational, orthostatic) from the dangerous (cardiac, structural, arrhythmic). High-risk features prompting admission for telemetry, transthoracic echocardiogram and electrophysiology review include age over 60, known or suspected structural heart disease, exertional or supine syncope, syncope causing serious injury, absent prodrome, family history of sudden cardiac death under 50, and any abnormal ECG (bifascicular block, prolonged QT, Brugada pattern, ventricular pre-excitation, pathological Q waves, hypertrophic features). Convulsive jerks during a syncopal episode reflect transient cerebral hypoperfusion and do not constitute epilepsy. Austroads imposes specific non-driving periods after syncope: 4 weeks after unexplained loss of consciousness, 4 weeks after a single vasovagal episode if recurrent or with injury, and longer periods after documented arrhythmia (variable by cause). Always ask about driving and document the advice given.
key_points: Cardiac syncope: abrupt, no prodrome, supine/exertional, abnormal ECG
Vasovagal: prodrome (warmth, nausea, blurred vision), upright trigger
Bifascicular block + syncope suggests intermittent CHB until proven otherwise
Austroads imposes non-driving periods after cardiac/unexplained syncope
explanation.pearls: Cardiac syncope red flags mandating admission with telemetry and TTE: age over 60, structural heart disease, exertional or supine syncope, absent prodrome, family history of sudden death, or abnormal ECG (bifascicular block, prolonged QT, Brugada pattern). Austroads imposes a 4-week non-driving period after unexplained cardiac syncope (Austroads 2022).

sources.label: ESC 2018 Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope

subtopic_detail: Brugada syndrome - type 1 ECG with syncope
stem: A 34-year-old male software engineer of South-East Asian background is brought to a tertiary hospital emergency department after a single witnessed episode of syncope at 0300 hours while sleeping next to his wife. His wife reports that he became briefly stiff for around 10 seconds with gasping agonal-type respirations and then recovered spontaneously over 1 minute with no confusion or post-event lethargy. He drinks approximately 4 standard drinks twice weekly, takes no regular medications, and has had a non-specific febrile illness over the previous 48 hours with paracetamol use for symptomatic relief of myalgia. He has had no previous episodes of syncope, pre-syncope, or palpitations. Family history is notable: a paternal first cousin died suddenly at age 28 during sleep with no clear cause identified at coronial autopsy; his father is well at age 62 with no cardiac history. Examination today is unremarkable; he is fully oriented with no neurological deficit. BP 124/72, HR 64 sinus, RR 14, T 37.8 degrees Celsius (febrile), SpO2 99% room air. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates a coved-type ST elevation of 2.5 mm in V1 and V2 with negative T waves and a J point >=2 mm, type 1 pattern present in two right precordial leads, persisting on repeat ECG with leads in standard and high (second intercostal space) positions. The QTc interval is 410 ms (normal). Cardiac troponin, electrolytes, magnesium and TSH are all within reference range. Echocardiogram is structurally normal with no regional wall motion abnormality. Urine toxicology screen is negative. There is no history of cocaine, tricyclic antidepressant, or sodium channel blocker exposure.

data_table.ECG: Type 1 Brugada pattern: coved ST elevation >=2 mm with negative T in V1 and V2. QTc 410 ms.
data_table.Family Hx: Paternal cousin sudden death age 28 during sleep.
data_table.Investigations: Troponin, electrolytes, Mg, TSH normal. Echo structurally normal. Tox screen negative.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate definitive management?

options.text: Reassure, advise paracetamol for fever, and arrange outpatient cardiology review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Spontaneous type 1 Brugada with syncope of likely arrhythmic origin plus a family history of sudden death is the highest-risk phenotype, requiring definitive therapy not outpatient deferral. This pathway would be correct only for an asymptomatic incidental type 2/3 Brugada pattern without high-risk features or family history (CSANZ/HRS Brugada statement; BMJ Best Practice).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Refer for implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) implantation under the symptomatic Brugada syndrome Class I indication
options.rationale: Symptomatic Brugada syndrome with spontaneous type 1 ECG and unexplained syncope likely arrhythmic in origin carries a Class I indication for ICD per the HRS/EHRA/APHRS expert consensus (endorsed by CSANZ) and is reflected in BMJ Best Practice. Family history of sudden death adds to risk. Fever (which unmasks/exacerbates Brugada) should be aggressively treated with paracetamol as adjunct, but does not obviate the indication for definitive therapy. Quinidine and EP-guided VT ablation are alternatives in some scenarios but ICD remains primary.
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Commence flecainide 100 mg twice daily for arrhythmia suppression
options.rationale: Flecainide is a Class IC sodium channel blocker that UNMASKS Brugada (it is the provocative challenge agent) and would precipitate ventricular arrhythmia. Flecainide is never correct in Brugada; the broader anti-pattern is reaching for sodium-channel blockers in any unexplained right-precordial ST elevation (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Commence amiodarone 200 mg daily for arrhythmia suppression
options.rationale: Amiodarone has no proven mortality benefit in Brugada and does not substitute for an ICD. Quinidine is the antiarrhythmic with the most Brugada-specific evidence and is used as ICD adjunct for recurrent shocks or storm. Amiodarone would be inappropriate even adjunctively (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

options.text: Arrange genetic testing for SCN5A and other Brugada-associated variants, and await the result before deciding on further definitive management
options.rationale: Genetic testing informs cascade screening of relatives but ~70 to 80% of probands have no identified pathogenic SCN5A variant, so a negative result does not exclude Brugada and would not change management of a clinically diagnosed symptomatic patient. Genetic testing is appropriate concurrently, not as a delay to ICD (CSANZ/HRS).
source_refs: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

explanation.summary: Brugada syndrome diagnosis requires spontaneous type 1 ECG (coved ST elevation >=2 mm in >=1 right precordial lead V1 or V2) plus a clinical feature (documented VF/polymorphic VT, syncope of probable arrhythmic origin, nocturnal agonal respiration, family history of sudden death <45, type 1 ECG in family member). Symptomatic Brugada has Class I ICD indication. Class IC drugs and fever provoke the syndrome.
key_points: Type 1 Brugada pattern + syncope = ICD (Class I)
Avoid sodium channel blockers (flecainide, propafenone) and TCAs
Fever unmasks Brugada - treat aggressively with paracetamol
Quinidine is the antiarrhythmic of choice as ICD adjunct or bridge
explanation.pearls: Look for spontaneous (not just drug-induced) type 1 pattern in any young adult with nocturnal syncope or sudden death family history (CSANZ/HRS).

sources.label: CSANZ/HRS-EHRA-APHRS Consensus on Inherited Primary Arrhythmia Syndromes

stem: A 46-year-old woman is referred to a metropolitan hypertension clinic with progressively worsening hypertension that has resisted intensification over 18 months. Her home blood pressure log, taken with a validated upper-arm device twice daily, averages 162/96 mmHg despite perindopril 10 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and indapamide 2.5 mg taken daily with documented adherence on pharmacy refill records over the past 12 months. She has no obstructive sleep apnoea symptoms (no snoring, no witnessed apnoeas, Epworth 4), no flushing or palpitations, no abdominal bruit on examination, and no family history of phaeochromocytoma, paraganglioma or thyroid medullary carcinoma. She has had intermittent hypokalaemia (lowest documented potassium 3.1 mmol/L) attributed by her referring GP to indapamide. Her most recent UEC shows: Na 142, K 3.4, Cl 102, HCO3 28 mmol/L, urea 5.6, creatinine 72 micromol/L, eGFR 89. Fasting glucose 5.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 5.7%. TSH and free T4 within reference range. Urinary catecholamine and metanephrine screen normal on two separate 24-hour collections. Renal artery Doppler ultrasonography shows no evidence of renal artery stenosis bilaterally. ECG shows left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria (Sokolow-Lyon positive). She is keen to investigate a potentially reversible secondary cause given her young age and target-organ damage on ECG.

data_table.BP: Home average 162/96 on perindopril 10, amlodipine 10, indapamide 2.5.
data_table.Bloods: Na 142, K 3.4 (low end), HCO3 28, Cr 72, eGFR 89, HbA1c 5.7%, TSH normal.
data_table.Other workup: Urinary catecholamines normal, renal Doppler no RAS, ECG LVH.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: 24-hour urinary metanephrines (repeat)
options.rationale: Phaeochromocytoma screening has been done with normal urinary catecholamines and no paroxysmal symptoms; repeating is low-yield. Repeat metanephrines would be correct if she developed new paroxysmal headaches/palpitations/diaphoresis or an adrenal incidentaloma with imaging features suggestive of phaeo (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: MR angiography of renal arteries
options.rationale: Renovascular hypertension has been excluded by Doppler and she lacks supportive features (abdominal bruit, flash pulmonary oedema, ACEi-induced AKI, asymmetric kidney sizes). MRA would be correct only if Doppler were equivocal in a high pre-test probability patient or fibromuscular dysplasia were suspected (younger woman) (BMJ Best Practice; Endocrine Society).

options.text: Overnight 1 mg dexamethasone suppression test
options.rationale: Cushing screening is justified by clinical features of hypercortisolism (central obesity, striae, easy bruising, proximal myopathy, glucose intolerance) - none described and HbA1c is normal. Dexamethasone suppression would be correct in resistant HTN plus Cushingoid features or recent rapid weight gain with new diabetes (Endocrine Society).

options.text: Plasma aldosterone-to-renin ratio after appropriate medication adjustment
options.rationale: Primary aldosteronism prevalence in resistant hypertension is 15 to 20% and is the most common reversible cause. The Endocrine Society and BMJ Best Practice recommend ARR screening in resistant HTN, spontaneous or diuretic-induced hypokalaemia, HTN with adrenal incidentaloma, or HTN <40 with end-organ damage. Pre-test preparation: replace potassium, normalise sodium, and ideally substitute interfering drugs (MRAs stopped for 4 to 6 weeks; ACEi/ARB/diuretics may falsely lower ratio but ARR is generally still interpretable with knowledge of medications). She has resistant HTN and diuretic-associated hypokalaemia: a textbook ARR indication.
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

options.text: Add spironolactone 25 mg empirically and reassess BP at 12 weeks
options.rationale: Empirical spironolactone is reasonable as fourth-line add-on in resistant HTN, but starting it before ARR will suppress the test for 4 to 6 weeks and miss a diagnosis with potentially curative implications (adrenalectomy in unilateral disease). This would be correct only after a primary aldosteronism workup is complete or declined (Endocrine Society).
source_refs: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

explanation.summary: Screen for primary aldosteronism (ARR) in resistant HTN, spontaneous/diuretic-induced hypokalaemia, HTN + adrenal incidentaloma, HTN with family history of early CVA/PA, or HTN <40 with end-organ damage. Confirm with saline suppression or fludrocortisone suppression, then adrenal CT and adrenal vein sampling if surgery considered.
key_points: PA causes 15 to 20% of resistant HTN
Screen with morning ARR after K replacement
Stop MRAs 4 to 6 weeks before testing
Lateralised disease (Conn adenoma) is surgically curable
explanation.pearls: Resistant HTN + low K = Conn's until proven otherwise; do the ARR before adding spironolactone (Endocrine Society).

sources.label: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: The Management of Primary Aldosteronism

subtopic_detail: NHFA hypertension - lifestyle as first-line for grade 1 low-risk
stem: A 38-year-old man presents to his GP for a workplace medical. He is asymptomatic, exercises twice a week, drinks 8 standard drinks weekly, has BMI 28, does not smoke, has no diabetes, and no family history of premature CVD. Three separate clinic BP readings over 6 weeks average 156/96, and home BP averaged over 7 days is 152/94. Bloods: total cholesterol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, triglycerides 1.2, fasting glucose 5.1, eGFR over 90, normal urea/electrolytes and urine ACR. ECG normal. His Australian absolute 5-year CV risk (NVDPA calculator) is 4 percent. He has tried 12 weeks of structured lifestyle change with his GP including reduced salt, DASH-style diet, alcohol reduction to 8 standard drinks per week, and increased exercise; BP today remains 154/94.

data_table.Bloods: Chol 4.6, LDL 2.8, HDL 1.4, TG 1.2, FBG 5.1, eGFR >90, ACR normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence perindopril 5 mg daily and continue ongoing lifestyle measures, with home BP review at 6 weeks
options.rationale: After an adequate trial of lifestyle change (3 to 6 months) without BP control, NHFA 2016 recommends adding pharmacotherapy in grade 1 to 2 hypertension even at low absolute CV risk. First-line agents are ACE inhibitor, ARB, dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker, or thiazide; perindopril is a reasonable first choice in a younger patient without contraindications (NHFA; eTG).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Continue lifestyle modification for another 6 months before considering pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: An adequate 3 to 6 month lifestyle trial has already failed at sustained BP above 140/90; further deferral risks ongoing end-organ exposure without proportional benefit (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Refer for 24-hour ambulatory BP monitoring to exclude white-coat hypertension before any pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Clinic and home readings are concordant for sustained hypertension; ABPM is unnecessary when these two agree, and adds delay (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Commence atorvastatin 20 mg daily for global CV risk reduction in addition to lifestyle
options.rationale: His absolute CV risk is low (4 percent) and lipids are unremarkable; statin is not indicated and does not address his uncontrolled BP (NVDPA; NHFA).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

options.text: Investigate for secondary hypertension with plasma aldosterone-renin ratio, renal artery imaging and 24-hour urinary metanephrines today
options.rationale: Secondary hypertension workup is reserved for resistant hypertension (BP uncontrolled on three agents including a diuretic), young-onset severe hypertension with red flags (hypokalaemia, episodic symptoms), or clinical clues; not first-line at this stage (NHFA 2016).
source_refs: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

explanation.summary: Grade 1 HTN (140 to 159/90 to 99) at low absolute CV risk: 3 to 6 months of lifestyle (DASH-style diet, salt <5 g/day, alcohol limits, exercise, weight reduction) then add pharmacotherapy if uncontrolled. Higher risk or higher BP grade triggers earlier or immediate medication.
key_points: Use absolute CV risk (NVDPA calculator) to guide pharmacotherapy threshold
DASH diet, exercise, weight loss, salt and alcohol limits
Reassess at 3 to 6 months for low-risk
Pharmacotherapy first-line: ACEi/ARB, dihydropyridine CCB, or thiazide
explanation.pearls: Absolute CV risk - not BP number alone - drives the decision to start drugs (NHFA 2016).

sources.label: NHFA 2016 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Hypertension in Adults

subtopic_detail: Complete heart block with haemodynamic compromise - immediate management
stem: A 74-year-old man is brought to a metropolitan emergency department by paramedic ambulance after a witnessed syncopal episode at home. His wife reports that he stood from his armchair, became pale, and collapsed without warning, hitting his head lightly on the carpeted floor; he was unresponsive for approximately 90 seconds before recovering some awareness. On arrival to the resuscitation bay he is confused (GCS 13: E4 V3 M6), cool peripherally, pale and diaphoretic. Vital signs at triage: HR 28, BP 76/40, RR 22, SpO2 95% on room air, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, blood glucose 7.8 mmol/L on point-of-care testing. Capillary refill is prolonged at 4 seconds. The 12-lead ECG demonstrates complete atrioventricular dissociation with an atrial rate of 84 and a ventricular escape rhythm of 28 with broad QRS complexes (160 ms) and a left bundle branch block morphology consistent with an infra-nodal escape. There are no acute ischaemic ST changes and no Q waves. He has no known cardiac history. His regular medications are amlodipine 10 mg daily and metformin 1 g twice daily. He has had no recent illness, no diarrhoea(no recent prescription changes, no access to other medications), and no environmental exposures. Initial bloods drawn at arrival: troponin pending, potassium 4.4 mmol/L, glucose 7.8, urea and creatinine within normal limits, calcium and magnesium normal.

data_table.Vitals: HR 28, BP 76/40, RR 22, SpO2 95% room air, GCS 13.
data_table.Examination: Cool peripherally, CRT 4 s, pale, diaphoretic, confused.
data_table.ECG: Complete AV dissociation. Atrial rate 84, ventricular escape rate 28, broad QRS 160 ms (LBBB morphology). No acute ischaemic changes.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention while awaiting cardiology?

options.text: IV adrenaline 1 mg bolus stat and prepare for immediate rapid sequence intubation as the airway intervention takes priority over rate restoration
options.rationale: 1 mg adrenaline is the cardiac arrest dose; for symptomatic bradycardia ANZCOR recommends adrenaline INFUSION 2 to 10 micrograms/min or 20 to 50 microgram boluses. A 1 mg bolus risks hypertensive crisis, intracerebral haemorrhage and tachyarrhythmia. Immediate intubation is not indicated while perfusion can be restored by pacing. The 1 mg dose would only be correct in confirmed cardiac arrest (ANZCOR adult ALS).

options.text: Atropine 600 micrograms IV, repeated every 3 to 5 minutes to a maximum cumulative dose of 3 mg, as the sole pharmacological strategy while awaiting cardiology review
options.rationale: Atropine is generally ineffective in CHB with a wide infra-nodal escape; ANZCOR explicitly recommends moving to pacing or chronotropic infusion. The anti-pattern is exhausting 3 mg atropine while the patient deteriorates. Atropine alone would be correct only as a brief trial in narrow-QRS (nodal) symptomatic bradycardia where AV node responsiveness is plausible (ANZCOR).

options.text: Commence transcutaneous pacing with analgesia/sedation, set rate 70 to 80, increase output until electrical and mechanical capture confirmed, while arranging emergent transvenous pacing
options.rationale: ANZCOR Adult Bradycardia algorithm: in haemodynamically compromised bradycardia unresponsive to atropine or where atropine is unlikely to work (infra-nodal CHB with wide escape), transcutaneous pacing is first-line emergency intervention. Confirm BOTH electrical capture (pacing spike followed by QRS) AND mechanical capture (palpable pulse or arterial tracing). Provide adequate sedation/analgesia (TCP is painful). Arrange definitive transvenous pacing followed by permanent pacemaker. Atropine can be tried concurrently but TCP should not be delayed.
source_refs: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT

options.text: 1 L 0.9% saline bolus over 15 minutes to optimise preload and reassess perfusion and conscious state before considering rate-restoring measures
options.rationale: Fluid will not address the conduction problem and risks pulmonary oedema in an elderly patient. Restoring rate is the definitive treatment. Fluid bolus would be correct only if hypotension were clearly driven by volume depletion with a normal underlying conduction system (ANZCOR; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% solution given over 10 minutes empirically while awaiting biochemistry, with repeat ECG at 5 minutes
options.rationale: Calcium is for confirmed/suspected hyperkalaemia or calcium channel blocker/beta-blocker toxicity. K is 4.4 mmol/L and there is no toxicology history. Empirical calcium would be correct only with widened QRS plus peaked T waves of hyperkalaemia, dialysis miss, or known CCB overdose (ANZCOR; Therapeutic Guidelines).

explanation.summary: ANZCOR adult bradycardia algorithm: if signs of life-threatening haemodynamic compromise, atropine 600 microgram IV (may repeat) as initial pharmacological step, but for wide-QRS infra-nodal block atropine often fails - escalate immediately to transcutaneous pacing (with sedation) or chronotropic infusion (adrenaline 2 to 10 micrograms/min, isoprenaline). Arrange transvenous pacing then permanent device.
key_points: Always confirm BOTH electrical and mechanical capture during TCP
Atropine often ineffective in wide-QRS infra-nodal block
Adrenaline infusion 2 to 10 mcg/min is an alternative when pacing unavailable
Identify and treat reversible causes (ischaemia, hyperkalaemia, drugs)
explanation.pearls: Mechanical capture (palpable pulse) is the goal, not just pacing spikes on the monitor (ANZCOR).

sources.label: ESC 2021 Guidelines on Cardiac Pacing and CRT
